title: FINE-TUNING CAN DISTORT PRETRAINED FEATURES - AND UNDERPERFORM OUT OF-DISTRIBUTION [PDF]

paragraphs.text:
Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

## FINE-TUNING CAN DISTORT PRETRAINED FEATURES - AND UNDERPERFORM OUT OF-DISTRIBUTION

## **Ananya Kumar, Aditi Raghunathan, Robbie Jones, Tengyu Ma, Percy Liang**

Stanford University, Computer Science Department

## ABSTRACT

When transferring a pretrained model to a downstream task, two popular methods are full fine-tuning (updating all the model parameters) and linear probing (updating only the last linear layer—the “head”). It is well known that fine-tuning leads to better accuracy in-distribution (ID). However, in this paper, we find that finetuning can achieve worse accuracy than linear probing out-of-distribution (OOD) when the pretrained features are good and the distribution shift is large. On 10 distribution shift datasets (BREEDS-Living17, BREEDS-Entity30, DomainNet, CIFAR _→_ STL, CIFAR-10.1, FMoW, ImageNetV2, ImageNet-R, ImageNet-A, ImageNet-Sketch), fine-tuning obtains on average 2% higher accuracy ID but 7% lower accuracy OOD than linear probing. We show theoretically that this tradeoff between ID and OOD accuracy arises even in a simple setting: fine-tuning overparameterized two-layer linear networks. Our analysis suggests that the easy two-step strategy of linear probing then full fine-tuning (LP-FT), sometimes used as a fine-tuning heuristic, combines the benefits of both fine-tuning and linear probing. Empirically, LP-FT outperforms both fine-tuning and linear probing on the above datasets (1% better ID, 10% better OOD than full fine-tuning).

## 1 INTRODUCTION

Pretraining a model on a large dataset before transferring to a downstream task’s training data substantially improves accuracy over training from scratch—for example, pretraining a ResNet-50 on unlabeled ImageNet boosts accuracy on CIFAR-10 from 94% to 98% (Chen et al., 2020a;b). High-stakes applications such as poverty mapping in under-resourced countries (Jean et al., 2016), self-driving cars (Yu et al., 2020), and medical diagnosis (AlBadawy et al., 2018), require models that also generalize to circumstances not seen in the training distribution. In addition to testing on data drawn from the downstream task’s training distribution (in-distribution; ID), it is increasingly important to test on data distributions unseen during training (out-of-distribution; OOD).

After initializing with a pretrained model, two popular transfer methods are fine-tuning (running gradient descent on all the model parameters), and linear probing (tuning the head but freezing lower layers). In the ID setting it is well known that fine-tuning leads to better accuracy than linear probing (Kornblith et al., 2019; Zhai et al., 2020; He et al., 2020), and even when testing OOD, prior work usually fine-tunes all parameters of their model (Hendrycks et al., 2019a; Miller et al., 2021; Andreassen et al., 2021). Intuitively, fine-tuning all layers of a network can improve pretrained features by adapting them to the specific task, while linear probing freezes these features.

In this work, we investigate the OOD accuracy of fine-tuning and linear probing and find that surprisingly, fine-tuning can do _worse_ than linear probing in the presence of a large distribution shift. We experiment on ten distribution shift benchmarks (BREEDS Living17, BREEDS Entity30, DomainNet, CIFAR _→_ STL, CIFAR10.1, FMoW Geo-shift, ImageNetV2, ImageNet-R, ImageNet-A, ImageNet-Sketch), initializing with good pretrained features from MoCo-v2 (Chen et al., 2020b) and CLIP (Radford et al., 2021). While both methods offer gains over training from scratch, fine-tuning improves the average ID accuracy relative to linear probing from 83% to 85% but brings down the OOD accuracy from 66% to 59% (Figure 1).

When and why does fine-tuning underperform linear probing? We theoretically consider fine-tuning a two-layer linear network in an overparameterized regression setting where the feature extractor layer has been pretrained to map high-dimensional inputs to useful, lower-dimensional, features. We prove that fine-tuning is worse than linear probing on directions outside the span of the training data when using “good” pretrained features. Even with an infinitesimally small learning rate, fine-tuning distorts pretrained features—the features of ID training data are updated while those of OOD data

(c) Our theory indicates that fine-tuning can distort the pretrained feature extractor and lead to poor OOD accuracy, but initializing with a linear probed head can fix this—empirically LP-FT gets better accuracies both ID and OOD.

change less. Since the head and feature extractor are simultaneously optimized during fine-tuning to a configuration that works well on ID training data, the head only accomodates the distorted features of ID points and performs poorly (relative to linear probing) on the less changed features of OOD points. Interestingly, we show that this feature distortion issue cannot be simply fixed by early stopping—throughout the entire process of fine-tuning, we never pass through parameters that do well OOD (relative to linear probing). On the other hand, given “good” features, linear probing extrapolates better OOD because it preserves pretrained features, but does worse than fine-tuning ID because linear probing cannot adapt the features to the downstream task.

**Technical challenges.** Existing theoretical work on transfer learning focuses on linear probing (Wu et al., 2020; Tripuraneni et al., 2020; Du et al., 2020). In contrast, analyses of fine-tuning is scarce and challenging because it requires understanding the training dynamics, instead of only the loss function and its global minimizers. In fact, fine-tuning and training from scratch optimize the _same_ training loss and only differ in their initializations (pretrained vs random). A mathematical analysis that distinguishes them needs to capture properties of the different minima that these algorithms converge to, a phenomenon that is sometimes theoretically referred to as the implicit regularization effect of initialization (Neyshabur et al., 2014). Accordingly, our analysis reasons about the parameters that gradient methods pass through starting from the pretrained initialization, which is challenging because this is a non-convex optimization problem and there is no known closed form for this trajectory. Two-layer linear networks are widely studied in the literature on implicit regularization (Saxe et al., 2014; Gunasekar et al., 2017; Gidel et al., 2019; Arora et al., 2018). However, they analyze random and often small initializations, which don’t capture pretraining.

**Algorithmic implications.** Our theory shows that fine-tuning underpeforms because when trying to fit ID training data with a randomly initialized head, the feature extractor changes significantly for ID examples, making features for ID and OOD examples largely inconsistent. This can be fixed by initializing with a good head that does not need to be updated much during fine-tuning, reducing how much the feature extractor changes. This suggests a simple two-step strategy of first linear probing to find a good head and then full fine-tuning (LP-FT). _Empirically, LP-FT outperforms fine-tuning and linear probing, both ID and OOD_ . Even on CIFAR-10.1 (small distribution shift), where fine-tuning is better for both ID and OOD, we find LP-FT outperforms fine-tuning on both metrics. LP-FT and vanilla fine-tuning use similar amounts of compute because the first step of linear probing is relatively very cheap. Prior work has used LP-FT (Levine et al., 2016; Kanavati & Tsuneki, 2021) (or variants such as layerwise fine-tuning (Howard & Ruder, 2018) or larger learning rates for the head layer (Prabhu et al., 2021))—however it has not been used for robustness / OOD accuracy, and we show that it addresses the ID-OOD tradeoff theoretically and empirically. Note that LP-FT is not meant to be a SOTA method but rather a simple, principled way to get good ID and OOD accuracy—we hope our analysis inspires even better methods for robust fine-tuning.

**Empirical validation.** Finally, we find that fine-tuning fails and LP-FT works, for the reasons predicted by our feature distortion theory: (1) fine-tuning changes the features for ID examples more than for OOD examples, leading to distortions; (2) LP-FT indeed changes both ID and OOD features 10 _−_ 100 _×_ less than fine-tuning does; (3) LP-FT gets the best of both worlds, achieving better accuracies than fine-tuning and linear probing both ID and OOD (Figure 1).

## 2 SETUP

**Task and evaluation.** Given training examples sampled from some distribution _P_ id, our goal is to learn a predictor _f_ : R _[d] →Y_ to map inputs _x ∈_ R _[d]_ to outputs _y ∈Y_ . We evaluate predictors on their standard “in-distribution” (ID) performance _L_ id on new test samples drawn from _P_ id that the training data is also sampled from. We also evaluate classifiers on their “out-of-distribution” (OOD) performance _L_ ood on test samples drawn from a new distribution _P_ ood that is different from _P_ id. Formally, for some loss function _ℓ_ , we evaluate classifiers on:

**Models.** In this work, we focus on predictors that leverage pretrained representations. We parameterize the final predictor _f_ as follows: given features _gB_ ( _x_ ) _∈_ R _[k]_ for some feature extractor parameters _B ∈B_ , and a linear “head” _v ∈V_ , we have _fv,B_ ( _x_ ) = _v[⊤] gB_ ( _x_ ). In our experiments (Section 4), _gB_ is a deep network and in our theory (Section 3), _gB_ is a linear projection.

We assume access to some initial pretrained feature extractor _B_ 0 that is obtained by training on potentially large amounts of data from a distribution that contains unlabeled or weakly supervised _x_ inputs from _P_ id and _P_ ood. We focus on two popular methods to learn a predictor _fv,B_ given training data from _P_ id: (i) linear probing where _B_ = _B_ 0 and the linear head is obtained by minimizing some loss (e.g., logistic loss for classification, squared loss for regression) on the training data, and (ii) fine-tuning where both _v_ and _B_ are updated by performing gradient descent on some loss on the training data with _B_ initialized at _B_ 0.

## 3 THEORY: FINE-TUNING DISTORTS PRETRAINED FEATURES

Our goal is to understand under what conditions fine-tuning does worse than linear probing out-of-distribution (OOD). We consider a linear setting (feature extractor _gB_ is linear) where the pretrained features are “good” and the OOD shift is large (Section 3.1). We prove our main result: that fine-tuning, in which all model parameters are updated, distorts features and gets suboptimal OOD error (Section 3.2, Theorem 3.2). We use this result to show that linear probing gets better OOD error but worse ID error than fine-tuning (Section 3.3). Finally, we explain why linear probing then fine-tuning can mitigate this ID-OOD tradeoff (Section 3.4).

Our analysis handles two key challenges which distinguishes it from prior work on transfer learning in linear models (Wu et al., 2020; Tripuraneni et al., 2020; Du et al., 2020; Xie et al., 2021a). Prior work focuses on linear probing, while we study fine-tuning where the resulting optimization problem is _non-convex_ . We also study _overparameterized models_ where the training loss alone does not determine test performance—this captures the fact that both training neural networks from scratch and fine-tuning them have the same training loss but very different test performance. However, it also makes the analysis challenging because we need to reason about the trajectory of gradient methods starting from a pretrained initialization, which has no known closed form.

3.2 FINE-TUNING DISTORTS PRETRAINED FEATURES

The more common method of using a pretrained feature extractor is fine-tuning (FT) which typically improves ID performance relative to linear probing (LP). In this section, we show that FT can distort features leading to poor OOD performance. We first explain the key intuitions and then present our formal theorem lower bounding the OOD error of FT (Section 3.2.2).

3.2.1 KEY INTUITIONS

We use two main observations to characterize when and why FT has higher OOD error than LP.

_1. Features get distorted: representations change only in the ID subspace (i.e., subspace spanned by the training data) and are unchanged in the orthogonal subspace._ To see this, we take the derivative of the training loss _L_[�] ( _v,B_ )= _∥XB[⊤] v−Y ∥_ 2[2][with respect to the feature extractor parameter] _[ B]_[:]

In Figure 2, if _B_ 0 is along the _x_ -axis (the ID subspace), then updating the features exclusively along the _x_ -axis would simply scale the initial features. In this case linear probing and fine-tuning will have identical behavior. However, if the angle between _B_ 0 and the _x_ -axis is non-zero, the updates would lead to distortions. ## 3.4 LINEAR PROBING THEN FINE-TUNING: A SIMPLE VARIANT TO MITIGATE TRADEOFFS
The advantage of fine-tuning is it can adapt the feature extractor to fit the downstream task. Can we keep this benefit while ensuring that our OOD error is low when we have good pretrained features?

Going back to Theorem 3.2, we see that the alignment error in the head initialization _φ_[2] = _|_ ( _v_ 0 _[⊤][v][⋆]_[)][2] _[−]_[(] _[v] ⋆[⊤][v][⋆]_[)][2] _[|]_[plays][an][important][role.][The][issue][with][FT][was][that][under][random] or zero initialization, _φ_[2] is usually large and since the gradient updates to the feature extractor parameter are coupled with that of the head parameter, the features get distorted in a manner that increases the OOD error. This suggests that we should use a better head initialization—one obtained from linear probing. If the pretrained features are decent, a linear probed head would be much better aligned with _v⋆_ allowing the features to be updated in a manner that does not increase the OOD error much.

We formally prove this intuition in a simple setting where we have perfect pretrained features. Note that in this case, linear probing alone gets zero OOD error—so Proposition 3.6 is just a first cut result to illustrate that if initialized well, full fine-tuning does not distort features.

## 4 EXPERIMENTS

We run experiments on ten benchmark datasets with deep neural networks and see that given good pretrained features, fine-tuning (FT) does better ID but worse OOD than linear probing (LP). As predicted by the theory, we find that LP-FT does better than both methods. Finally, we see that a number of predictions from the feature distortion theory hold up in practice. For more details on datasets, pretraining models, and experiment protocols, see Appendix B.

We use standard distribution shift datasets: **DomainNet** (Peng et al., 2019; Tan et al., 2020), **BREEDS-Living-17** (Santurkar et al., 2020), **BREEDS-Entity-30** (Santurkar et al., 2020), **CIFAR-10** _→_ **STL** (Krizhevsky, 2009; Coates et al., 2011; French et al., 2018), **CIFAR-10** _→_ **CIFAR-10.1** (Recht et al., 2018), **ImageNet-1K** (Russakovsky et al., 2015)—where the OOD test sets are **ImageNetV2** (Recht et al., 2019), **ImageNet-R** (Hendrycks et al., 2020), **ImageNetA** (Hendrycks et al., 2019b), and **ImageNet-Sketch** (Wang et al., 2019)—, and **FMoW Geo-shift** which is adapted from the satellite remote sensing dataset _Functional Map of the World_ (Christie et al., 2018; Koh et al., 2021). See Appendix B for more details on the datasets.

**Pretraining and models.** We use a CLIP pretrained ViT-B/16 for ImageNet. For the other datasets we use a ResNet-50 architecture and consider a diverse range of pretraining methods and datasets: MoCo-v2 (Chen et al., 2020b), CLIP (Radford et al., 2021), and MoCo-TP (Ayush et al., 2020). In Appendix B, we also show results for a CLIP-ViT-B/16 and more fine-tuning baselines on Living-17.

## 4.1 LINEAR PROBING VS FINE-TUNING

**Experiment protocols.** We initialize with the pretrained model, and fine-tune or linear probe on ID training examples. For fine-tuning on each dataset we swept over 6 learning rates, using a cosine learning rate schedule and batch size of 64. We early stop and choose the best learning rate using ID validation accuracy. For linear probing we train an _ℓ_ 2-regularized logistic regression classifier on frozen features from the penultimate layer of the pretrained model, selecting the best _ℓ_ 2-regularization hyperparameter based on ID validation accuracy. For all methods, we run each hyperparameter configuration 3 times (with different random seeds), and take the average accuracy. We used a slightly different protocol for ImageNet because the dataset is much larger and running these experiments involves more computational resources: we used a batch size of 128, swept over 3 learning rates for both fine-tuning and linear probing (we did not sweep over _ℓ_ 2-regularization), and ran each hyperparameter configuration once. In all cases, OOD data was only used for evaluation.

**Results.** Fine-tuning (FT) does better than linear probing (LP) on 5 out of 6 ID datasets (average accuracy of 85.1% for FT vs. 82.9% for LP, see Table 1). This is consistent with prior work and intuitions. However, linear probing does better on 8 out of 10 OOD datasets (average accuracy of 66.2% for LP vs. 59.3% for FT, see Table 2)—LP does better on all datasets except CIFAR-10.1 and ImageNetV2, where the OOD is designed to closely replicate the ID dataset. This matches

## 4.2 LINEAR PROBING THEN FINE-TUNING (LP-FT)

**Experiment protocols.** For LP-FT, we initialize the neural network head using the linear probed solution, and then fine-tune the model. LP-FT and fine-tuning use similar compute because the linear probing step is much faster than fine-tuning. As with fine-tuning, we swept over 6 learning rates, early stopping using ID validation accuracy. For the ImageNet experiments we swept over 3 learning rates, and explicitly ensured that LP-FT and fine-tuning use exactly the same compute (we ran each stage of LP-FT for half as many epochs as we ran vanilla fine-tuning).

**Results.** We find that LP-FT gets the best accuracy ID (average: 85.7%) and OOD (average: 68.9%). This is true for 5/6 ID and 10/10 OOD datasets—every dataset except FMoW ID, where LP-FT is better than linear probing but worse than fine-tuning. Since the ID accuracy on FMoW is low (56.5%), this could be because the pretrained features are not good.

## 4.3 EXAMINING THE FEATURE DISTORTION THEORY

**Early stopping does not mitigate feature distortion.** Our theory predicts that fine-tuning can do worse OOD (than linear probing) throughout the process of fine-tuning, and not just at the end. To test this, we early stop each fine-tuning method and choose the best learning rate based on OOD test accuracy. As expected, fine-tuning does improve a little, but linear probing (average accuracy: 67.1%) is still better than fine-tuning (average accuracy: 61.3%). See Appendix B for per-dataset results.

**ID-OOD features get distorted from fine-tuning.** The feature distortion theory predicts that fine-tuning changes features for ID examples more than for OOD examples, which is why fitting a head on ID examples performs poorly OOD. To test this, for each example _x_ in Living-17 (results for other datasets are in Appendix B), we took the Euclidean distance of the ResNet-50 features before and after fine-tuning: _∥gB_ ( _x_ ) _−gB_ 0 ( _x_ ) _∥_ 2. As expected, the average distance for ID examples (0 _._ 0188 _±_ 0 _._ 0001) is more than for OOD examples (0 _._ 0167 _±_ 0 _._ 0001). The theory also predicts that LP-FT changes features less than fine-tuning does. As expected, the average distance changed by LP-FT both ID (0 _._ 0011 _±_ 0 _._ 0001) and OOD (0 _._ 0009 _±_ 0 _._ 0001) is 20 _×_ smaller than for fine-tuning.

**Pretrained features must be good, ID-OOD far apart.** Our theory says that linear probing does better than fine-tuning OOD, but only if the OOD and ID data are quite different, and the pretrained features are good—otherwise fine-tuning can do better OOD by adjusting the feature extractor ID.

_Feature quality_ : We use a checkpoint of MoCo-v1 that got 10% worse accuracy (on ImageNet) and compare linear probing and fine-tuning on Living-17. With worse features, both methods do worse, but fine-tuning (96% ID, 71% OOD) does better than linear probing (92% ID, 66% OOD).

_ID ≈ OOD_ : We fine-tune / linear probe on CIFAR-10, and test on CIFAR-10.1, a dataset collected using a similar protocol to CIFAR-10. As expected, fine-tuning (92.3%) outperforms linear probing OOD (82.7%). Even in this case, where we have no tradeoffs, LP-FT does the best (93.5%).

## 5 RELATED WORK AND DISCUSSION

**Fine-tuning vs. linear probing.** Fine-tuning (FT) and linear probing (LP) are popular transfer learning algorithms. There is substantial evidence of FT outperforming LP in-distribution (ID) including recent large-scale investigations (Kornblith et al., 2019; Chen et al., 2021; Zhai et al., 2020; Chen et al., 2020b) (the only notable exception is in Peters et al. (2019) where LP performs better than FT when using ELMo representations, but worse using BERT). FT is therefore the method of choice for improving accuracy, while LP is used to analyze properties of representations (Peters et al., 2018; Belinkov et al., 2017; Hewitt & Manning, 2019). In our work, we find that FT can underperform LP especially when using high quality pretrained features in the presence of a large distribution shift. There are a variety of other fine-tuning heuristics (Ge & Yu, 2017; Guo et al., 2019; Zhang et al., 2020; Zhu et al., 2020; Jiang et al., 2021; Aghajanyan et al., 2021)—combining our insights with these ideas might lead to better methods.

**The benefit of preserving pretrained features.** Our work adds to growing evidence that _lightweight_ fine-tuning, where only a small part of a pretrained model are updated, can perform better under distribution shifts—and we give a theoretical grounding to why this might be the case. Zero-shot language prompting in vision (Radford et al., 2021) and other lightweight fine-tuning approaches in NLP (Houlsby et al., 2019; Li & Liang, 2021; Xie et al., 2021b; Lester et al., 2021; Utama et al., 2021; Zhou et al., 2021) have been shown to improve OOD performance. Andreassen et al. (2021) observe that through the course of fine-tuning, ID accuracy increases but OOD accuracy plateaus.

**Mitigating ID-OOD tradeoffs.** While LP-FT has sometimes been used as a fine-tuning heuristic (Levine et al., 2016; Kanavati & Tsuneki, 2021; fastai), it has not been used for robustness / OOD accuracy, and we show that it addresses the ID-OOD tradeoff theoretically and empirically. Tradeoffs between ID and OOD accuracy are widely studied and prior work self-trains on large amounts of unlabeled data to mitigate such tradeoffs (Raghunathan et al., 2020; Xie et al., 2021a; Khani & Liang, 2021). In contrast, LP-FT uses no extra unlabeled data and is a simple variant of fine-tuning. In concurrent and independent work, Wortsman et al. (2021) show that ensembling the weights of a zero-shot and fine-tuned model mitigates the ID-OOD tradeoff between these approaches, and this method could be promising for our datasets as well.

**Theoretical analysis of transfer learning.** Prior works on transfer learning mainly analyze linear probing (Wu et al., 2020; Tripuraneni et al., 2020; Du et al., 2020). Recent works (Chua et al., 2021; Shachaf et al., 2021) study fine-tuning, but in the underparameterized regime (where there is a unique global optimum) or assuming a balanced initialization. Prior works also focus on ID error, while we analyzeOODerror. SeeSectionCforadditionalrelatedworkontheoryofoverparameterizedmodels.

## 6 CONCLUSION.

There is a strong trend towards leveraging pretrained models to improve downstream performance, and whenever feasible, it is common to fine-tune all model parameters. In this work, we show theoretically and empirically that preserving features might be important for robustness, and simpler approaches like linear probing can improve out-of-distribution (OOD) performance. _This OOD gap between fine-tuning and linear probing grows as the quality of pretrained features improve, so we believe our results are likely to gain significance over time with growing innovations and scale of pretraining_ .

Finally, we showed LP-FT can mitigate tradeoffs between ID and OOD accuracy in our context. LPFT could be useful in other situations, for example in CLIP we could initialize the final layer with the zero-shot classifier and then fine-tune the entire model, as done in concurrent work (Wortsman et al., 2021). In NLP, linear probing is not as good—here we could first prompt-tune (Lester et al., 2021) and then fine-tune the entire model. LP-FT is just a first step in leveraging the intuition from our theoretical analysis and we hope that this work inspires new methods of leveraging powerful pretrained models.

progressive__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-previ.label: Progressive (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
progressive__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-previ.overall_feedback: The paper is exceptionally well-structured, combining a clear, high-stakes research problem with a rigorous, two-pronged approach that bridges the gap between empirical observation and theoretical justification. The quality of the scholarship is high; the authors effectively synthesize the "fine-tuning vs. linear probing" trade-off and provide a compelling, actionable solution (LP-FT) that is supported by a broad range of benchmarks. The primary strength lies in the authors' ability to frame a counterintuitive empirical finding—that fine-tuning can lead to feature distortion and OOD underperformance—within the context of implicit regularization. The writing is precise, and the technical challenges are framed with refreshing honesty. One potential issue to monitor is the reliance on a two-layer linear model for the theoretical analysis; while this provides necessary tractability, the paper would benefit from a robust discussion later on regarding whether the insights regarding "feature distortion" scale intuitively to the non-linear, deep architectures used in the empirical experiments. Overall, this opening establishes a highly convincing case for a shift in standard transfer learning practices.

progressive__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.label: Progressive (qwen3.6-35b-a3b)
progressive__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.overall_feedback: This paper addresses a timely and practically significant question by rigorously challenging the conventional wisdom that full fine-tuning universally outperforms linear probing, particularly when models are evaluated under distribution shift. The quality of the work is high, seamlessly integrating comprehensive empirical evaluation across ten diverse OOD benchmarks with a theoretically grounded analysis of feature distortion in overparameterized linear networks, ultimately motivating a principled two-stage LP-FT strategy that empirically dominates both baselines on ID and OOD metrics. The manuscript is exceptionally well-organized and clearly written, effectively framing the problem, summarizing key empirical findings, and articulating the theoretical mechanism and algorithmic implications in a logical progression. The most significant issue, however, centers on the extrapolation of the simplified two-layer linear theory to modern deep feature extractors: while the linear analysis elegantly isolates how simultaneous optimization distorts ID-adapted features at the expense of OOD directions, it remains unclear how this mechanism interacts with the highly non-linear optimization landscapes, adaptive optimizers, and layer-wise implicit regularization of contemporary architectures, potentially limiting the theoretical insight’s direct transferability. Furthermore, while the LP-FT heuristic is compelling, the introduction’s claim of its broad superiority would benefit from earlier acknowledgment of potential hyperparameter sensitivity, computational overhead, and performance variations across tasks with minimal distribution shift, as the current framing risks overgeneralizing a promising strategy. Overall, the paper makes a strong conceptual and empirical contribution, but would be substantially strengthened by a more nuanced discussion of theoretical boundaries and broader empirical robustness checks to cement its practical recommendations.

comments.title: Ambiguity in quantifying feature change

comments.explanation: The empirical claim states that LP-FT changes features '10-100x less' than fine-tuning, but does not specify the metric used to quantify this 'change'. Given the theoretical focus on gradient flow dynamics and the coupled updates between the head and feature extractor, this likely refers to the magnitude of parameter updates or trajectory length during the fine-tuning phase. However, without clarifying whether this is measured via Frobenius norm of updates, final parameter distance from initialization, or another metric, a careful reader might misinterpret the scale or nature of the comparison. Defining the metric would strengthen the empirical validation.

A brief clarification that CIFAR-10.1 is treated here as a cleaner test distribution to probe label-shift robustness would improve terminological precision.

comments.title: Confusing singular value indexing in Definition 3.1
comments.quote: _Then_ cos _θ_ max( _A,B_ )= _σr_ ( _E[⊤] F_ ) _, which is the r-th largest singular value of E[⊤] F ._
comments.explanation: I initially wondered why the largest principal angle corresponds to `σ_r`. comments.title: Overstated gradient non-zero claim in intuition section
comments.quote: However, the gradient is non-zero for directions _u_ in the ID subspace and the corresponding features _Bu_ change across the fine-tuning process.
comments.explanation: While reviewing the intuition for feature distortion, I noticed the claim that the gradient is non-zero for all directions `u` in the ID subspace `S`. I computed the gradient `∇_B L = 2 X^⊤ r v^⊤`, where `r = X B^⊤ v - Y`. Its action on a direction `u` is `(X^⊤ r)(v^⊤ u)`. This directional derivative is zero whenever `v ⊥ u`, which can happen even if `u ∈ S`. The claim is only generically true (e.g., under random initialization where `v` is unlikely to be orthogonal to any specific basis of `S`). The statement should be qualified to note that the gradient acts on the projection of `v` onto `S`, rather than asserting it is strictly non-zero for all `u ∈ S`. As written, a careful reader might question the rigorous basis of the distortion argument.

comments.title: Typo in non-degeneracy conditions of Proposition 3.6
comments.quote: Under the non-degeneracy conditions_ cos _θ_ max( _R_ 0 _,S_ ) =0 _,_ cos _θ_ max( _R_ 0 _,S[⊥]_ ) =0 _:
comments.explanation: Initially, I was confused because the proposition claims non-degeneracy requires the cosines of the principal angles to be exactly 0. A cosine of 0 implies the subspaces are orthogonal, which would mean the pretrained features are completely misaligned with the training data span (and its complement), rendering gradient updates degenerate or trivial. This directly contradicts the requirement in Sections 3.2 and 3.3 that these cosines be *non-zero* (≠ 0) to ensure meaningful alignment. After checking the context, it is clear this is a typographical error where `=0` should be `≠ 0`. As written, the condition inverts the intended mathematical constraint and would invalidate the theorem's premises.

comments.title: Mismatch between informal prose and formal condition in OOD comparison
comments.quote: we require that the training data cannot be exactly in the same direction or orthogonal to the pretrained features, formally that cos _θ_ max( _R∗,S_ ) and cos _θ_ max( _R∗,S[⊥]_ ) are not 0 where _R∗_ = rowspace( _B⋆_ ).
comments.explanation: The informal prose states that training data 'cannot be exactly in the same direction' as the pretrained features. However, the formal condition only specifies that `cos θ_max ≠ 0`, which rules out orthogonality but permits the subspaces to be identical (where `cos θ_max = 1`). If $R^*$ and $S$ were identical, fine-tuning would simply scale the pretrained features rather than distort them, fundamentally changing the theoretical behavior regarding feature distortion. The formal statement thus fails to exclude the case mentioned in the prose, creating a minor ambiguity about whether exact alignment is theoretically permitted under the result.

comments.title: Extrapolation of theoretical gap growth to practical scaling scenarios
comments.quote: This OOD gap between fine-tuning and linear probing grows as the quality of pretrained features improve, so we believe our results are likely to gain significance over time with growing innovations and scale of pretraining
comments.explanation: Initially, the confident claim that the OOD gap will consistently grow with scale caught my attention. I checked the theoretical foundation and confirmed that Theorem 3.2 and the subsequent discussion around $\epsilon \to 0$ do support a widening gap under the authors' specific assumptions (e.g., fixed non-degeneracy conditions and head alignment error $\phi^2$). The authors correctly prove that FT's OOD error remains bounded away from zero due to coupled head-feature dynamics, while LP's vanishes with perfect features. However, what remains problematic is the unqualified leap to practical model scaling. Real-world pretraining and scaling involve simultaneous changes to initialization schemes, data distributions, and architectural inductive biases, which could interact with the non-degeneracy conditions or $\phi^2$ in non-monotonic ways. Adding a caveat that this trajectory holds provided initialization and distributional assumptions remain stable during scaling would strengthen this forward-looking statement.

comments.title: Broad generalization about prior work focusing on ID error
comments.quote: Prior works also focus on ID error, while we analyze OOD error.
comments.explanation: I was initially concerned by the blanket statement that prior transfer learning works focus on ID error while this paper analyzes OOD error, as generalization and robustness are central themes in much of the field. I checked the cited references (Wu et al., 2020; Tripuraneni et al., 2020; Du et al., 2020) and saw that while they rigorously analyze ID convergence and linear probing dynamics, their theoretical bounds and experimental setups are indeed primarily framed around in-distribution fitting. The authors correctly establish a novel mechanistic theory for *how* fine-tuning distorts features to hurt OOD performance, which prior LP-focused theory does not cover. Still, the phrasing slightly overstates the literature gap and could mislead readers into thinking prior work ignored OOD evaluation entirely. Clarifying that the paper's novelty lies in the mechanistic analysis of OOD distortion dynamics, rather than the mere fact of evaluating OOD error, would be more precise.

comments.title: Speculative extension to NLP without theoretical bridging
comments.quote: In NLP, linear probing is not as good—here we could first prompt-tune (Lester et al., 2021) and then fine-tune the entire model.
comments.explanation: The suggestion to use prompt-tuning before full fine-tuning in NLP initially seemed like a practical heuristic that diverges from the core theory. I reasoned through the analogy and recognized the authors are correctly identifying the shared benefit of decoupling task-component initialization from feature extraction, which aligns with the LP-FT intuition. However, prompt-tuning optimizes soft prompt vectors injected into input embeddings, which operates very differently mathematically from optimizing a final linear head $v$ coupled with a feature extractor $B$. The "balancedness" invariants and subspace distortion dynamics proven for overparameterized linear networks do not directly map to transformer prompt tuning. While the heuristic is empirically sound (as noted with concurrent CLIP work), the connection to the paper's theoretical mechanism is tenuous. Briefly acknowledging that the underlying principle may generalize but requires new theoretical tools to prove in the NLP/transformer setting would properly scope this suggestion.

progressive_original__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.label: Progressive Original (qwen3.6-35b-a3b)
progressive_original__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.overall_feedback: This paper addresses a timely and practically significant question by rigorously challenging the conventional wisdom that full fine-tuning universally outperforms linear probing, particularly when models are evaluated under distribution shift. The quality of the work is high, seamlessly integrating comprehensive empirical evaluation across ten diverse OOD benchmarks with a theoretically grounded analysis of feature distortion in overparameterized linear networks, ultimately motivating a principled two-stage LP-FT strategy that empirically dominates both baselines on ID and OOD metrics. The manuscript is exceptionally well-organized and clearly written, effectively framing the problem, summarizing key empirical findings, and articulating the theoretical mechanism and algorithmic implications in a logical progression. The most significant issue, however, centers on the extrapolation of the simplified two-layer linear theory to modern deep feature extractors: while the linear analysis elegantly isolates how simultaneous optimization distorts ID-adapted features at the expense of OOD directions, it remains unclear how this mechanism interacts with the highly non-linear optimization landscapes, adaptive optimizers, and layer-wise implicit regularization of contemporary architectures, potentially limiting the theoretical insight’s direct transferability. Furthermore, while the LP-FT heuristic is compelling, the introduction’s claim of its broad superiority would benefit from earlier acknowledgment of potential hyperparameter sensitivity, computational overhead, and performance variations across tasks with minimal distribution shift, as the current framing risks overgeneralizing a promising strategy. Overall, the paper makes a strong conceptual and empirical contribution, but would be substantially strengthened by a more nuanced discussion of theoretical boundaries and broader empirical robustness checks to cement its practical recommendations.

comments.title: Ambiguity in quantifying feature change
comments.quote: (2) LP-FT indeed changes both ID and OOD features 10 _−_ 100 _×_ less than fine-tuning does;
comments.explanation: The empirical claim states that LP-FT changes features '10-100x less' than fine-tuning, but does not specify the metric used to quantify this 'change'. Given the theoretical focus on gradient flow dynamics and the coupled updates between the head and feature extractor, this likely refers to the magnitude of parameter updates or trajectory length during the fine-tuning phase. However, without clarifying whether this is measured via Frobenius norm of updates, final parameter distance from initialization, or another metric, a careful reader might misinterpret the scale or nature of the comparison. Defining the metric would strengthen the empirical validation.

comments.title: Terminology regarding CIFAR-10.1 as a 'distribution shift'
comments.quote: Even on CIFAR-10.1 (small distribution shift), where fine-tuning is better for both ID and OOD, we find LP-FT outperforms fine-tuning on both metrics.
comments.explanation: CIFAR-10.1 is standardly recognized in the literature as a manually corrected test set for CIFAR-10, primarily used to evaluate robustness to label noise rather than as a generative domain shift in input space. While the authors reasonably use 'distribution shift' broadly to denote any test distribution differing from the training set, this phrasing could subtly mislead readers expecting an input-domain shift. A brief clarification that CIFAR-10.1 is treated here as a cleaner test distribution to probe label-shift robustness would improve terminological precision.

progressive__deepseek-v4-flash.label: Progressive (deepseek-v4-flash)
progressive__deepseek-v4-flash.overall_feedback: This paper presents a compelling and timely investigation into a critical yet underexplored phenomenon: that full fine-tuning can degrade out-of-distribution (OOD) accuracy relative to linear probing when pretrained features are strong. The empirical evidence across ten distribution shift benchmarks is robust and clearly demonstrates a consistent trade-off, and the proposed LP-FT method offers a practical, principled fix. The theoretical analysis using a two-layer linear network provides a clean mechanistic explanation for feature distortion and why early stopping fails, though the gap between this simplified model and deep nonlinear networks warrants further scrutiny—specifically, whether the assumed "good" features and linear dynamics hold in practice. The paper is well-organized and clearly written, with effective figures that visually summarize the core ideas. The most significant issue is that the theoretical results rely on an overparameterized linear regression setting with a specific pretrained structure, leaving open questions about how general the findings are for different architectures, optimizers, and pretraining strategies (e.g., supervised vs. self-supervised). Additionally, while the authors mention that LP-FT also works when fine-tuning is better OOD, a more detailed analysis of when and why the trade-off shifts (e.g., small vs. large distribution shifts) would strengthen the contribution. Overall, this is a high-quality paper that makes a strong empirical and theoretical case for rethinking standard fine-tuning practices.

comments.quote: _ where ϕ² = | (v_0^⊥ v_∗)^2 - (v_∗^⊥ v_∗)^2 | is defined to be inital head alignment error and ε ≥ d(B_0, B_∗) is the error in the pretrained feature extractor.

_This holds for all times t for FT (and therefore also for the limit v_ ft _[∞][,B]_ ft _[∞][) and the LP iterates converge] to v_ lp _[∞][,B]_[0] _[ as a result of the gradient flow on a convex problem.]_
comments.explanation: The statement of Theorem 3.4 ends abruptly after 'then,' with no inequality or conclusion visible. The following sentence appears to be commentary rather than the theorem's claim. The formal version is referenced to Appendix A.9, but an informal statement in the main text should include the core mathematical result (e.g., an upper or lower bound on OOD error). The missing formula makes it impossible to understand what is being asserted without going to the appendix. comments.title: Likely typo in non-degeneracy conditions of Proposition 3.6
comments.quote: Under the non-degeneracy conditions cos _θ_ max( _R_ 0 _,S_ ) =0 _,_ cos _θ_ max( _R_ 0 _,S[⊥]_ ) =0 _:
comments.explanation: Earlier in the same passage, 'non-degeneracy conditions' are described as requiring cos θ_max not equal to 0 (i.e., subspaces not orthogonal). Here, the conditions are written with '=0', which would force the subspaces to be orthogonal. Moreover, both conditions cannot hold simultaneously because S and S^⊥ are orthogonal complements; a non-zero subspace R_0 cannot be orthogonal to both unless it is trivial. This is almost certainly an OCR artifact where '≠' was misread as '='. The intended conditions are almost surely '≠0' (or '>0', as in Theorem 3.2). The error would lead a reader to believe the proposition applies under degenerate, impossible conditions, contradicting the surrounding text.

comments.title: Claim that LP-FT does best on all 10 OOD datasets contradicts table for Entity-30
comments.quote: LP-FT does the best on all 10 datasets.

However, in Table 2, the OOD accuracy for Entity-30 (Ent-30) is 63.2 for LP and 62.3 for LP-FT, meaning LP outperforms LP-FT on that dataset. The phrase 'all 10 datasets' is repeated in the next paragraph ('This is true for 5/6 ID and 10/10 OOD datasets'). Unless the table column is misaligned due to OCR (which is plausible given the repeated headers), the presented numbers directly contradict the claim. Since the paper otherwise provides correct overall averages (LP-FT average 68.9 > LP 66.2), the per‑dataset claim appears overstated. The contradiction would mislead a careful reader who checks the reported numbers.

comments.title: Potential OCR error in definition of head alignment error φ²
comments.explanation: The expression (v_⋆^⊤ v_⋆)^2 computes the fourth power of the norm of v_⋆, which is unusual for a head alignment error. Typically alignment error would involve a squared norm or a squared cosine, e.g., ‖v_⋆‖² - (v_0^⊤ v_⋆)²/‖v_⋆‖², or simply (‖v_⋆‖² - (v_0^⊤ v_⋆)²). The double squaring may be a typographical artifact from OCR (e.g., an extra superscript 2) or an author error. Without seeing the full proof it is hard to judge whether this definition is intended, but it stands out against standard conventions and could affect the interpretation of the lower bound. The authors correctly intend to measure alignment between v_0 and v_⋆, and the reasoning in the proof sketch is consistent with an error that grows when v_0 is far from v_⋆, so the issue is likely a notational glitch rather than a conceptual flaw.

Given that this is the first time the theorem is presented in the excerpt, the omission is significant.

comments.title: Likely typo in non-degeneracy conditions of Proposition 3.6
comments.quote: Under the non-degeneracy conditions cos _θ_ max( _R_ 0 _,S_ ) =0 _,_ cos _θ_ max( _R_ 0 _,S[⊥]_ ) =0 _:
comments.explanation: Earlier in the same passage, 'non-degeneracy conditions' are described as requiring cos θ_max not equal to 0 (i.e., subspaces not orthogonal). Here, the conditions are written with '=0', which would force the subspaces to be orthogonal. Moreover, both conditions cannot hold simultaneously because S and S^⊥ are orthogonal complements; a non-zero subspace R_0 cannot be orthogonal to both unless it is trivial. This is almost certainly an OCR artifact where '≠' was misread as '='. The intended conditions are almost surely '≠0' (or '>0', as in Theorem 3.2). The error would lead a reader to believe the proposition applies under degenerate, impossible conditions, contradicting the surrounding text.

comments.title: Claim that LP-FT does best on all 10 OOD datasets contradicts table for Entity-30
comments.quote: LP-FT does the best on all 10 datasets.
comments.explanation: The text states that LP-FT achieves the best OOD accuracy on every individual dataset. However, in Table 2, the OOD accuracy for Entity-30 (Ent-30) is 63.2 for LP and 62.3 for LP-FT, meaning LP outperforms LP-FT on that dataset. The phrase “all 10 datasets” is repeated in the next paragraph (“This is true for 5/6 ID and 10/10 OOD datasets”). Unless the table column is misaligned due to OCR (which is plausible given the repeated headers), the presented numbers directly contradict the claim. Since the paper otherwise provides correct overall averages (LP-FT average 68.9 > LP 66.2), the per‑dataset claim appears overstated. The contradiction would mislead a careful reader who checks the reported numbers.

progressive__glm-4.7-flash.label: Progressive (glm-4.7-flash)
progressive__glm-4.7-flash.overall_feedback: This paper presents a compelling counterintuitive finding that challenges standard transfer learning practices by demonstrating empirically and theoretically that full fine-tuning can severely degrade Out-of-Distribution (OOD) accuracy compared to linear probing, particularly under large distribution shifts. The empirical study is robust across ten diverse benchmarks, and the theoretical contribution offers valuable insight into implicit regularization and feature distortion in overparameterized settings, effectively ruling out early stopping as a solution. However, a significant limitation is the scope of the theoretical analysis, which is restricted to two-layer linear networks; while the results are motivated by modern deep architectures, it remains unclear whether the mechanisms of feature distortion and the effectiveness of the LP-FT strategy generalize to the non-linear, deep networks (e.g., ResNets, Transformers) used for empirical evaluation.

comments.title: Spelling/Notation Inconsistency: 'finetuning' vs 'fine-tuning'
comments.quote: However, in this paper, we find that finetuning can achieve worse accuracy than linear probing out-of-distribution (OOD)... Empirically, LP-FT outperforms both fine-tuning and linear probing
comments.explanation: The abstract consistently uses the hyphenated form 'fine-tuning' (e.g., 'full fine-tuning', 'better accuracy in-distribution (ID)'), but the phrase 'finetuning' appears once in the abstract and is used elsewhere without the hyphen (e.g., 'fine-tuning leads to better accuracy...'). While it is an obvious OCR or casual typing error, the context fully resolves the ambiguity for the reader; the authors clearly mean the hyperparameter fine-tuning technique. This is a minor formatting issue.

comments.title: Dataset Name Formatting Inconsistency
comments.quote: BREEDS-Living17, BREEDS-Entity30, DomainNet, CIFAR _→_ STL, CIFAR-10.1, FMoW, ImageNetV2, ImageNet-R, ImageNet-A, ImageNet-Sketch
comments.explanation: In the Abstract, dataset names use specific formatting: 'BREEDS-Living17' uses a hyphen, 'CIFAR _→_ STL' uses an arrow (likely a representation of LaTeX's \rightarrow), and 'CIFAR-10.1' uses a hyphen. In the Introduction, the list reads 'BREEDS Living17', 'BREEDS Entity30', 'CIFAR10.1'. There is a mix of hyphens vs. spaces (e.g., 'Living17' vs 'Living17') and variations in dataset identifier casing (e.g., 'CIFAR-10.1' vs 'CIFAR10.1'). These are likely OCR artifacts or intentional formatting differences, but they introduce slight notational inconsistency across the two lists.

comments.title: Incomplete Theorem Statement
comments.quote: then, This holds for all times t for FT (and therefore also for the limit v_ft^∞, B_ft^∞) and the LP iterates converge to v_lp^∞, B_0 as a result of the gradient flow on a convex problem.]
comments.explanation: The text for Theorem 3.4 contains a grammatical fragment. It lists the hypothesis (the non-degeneracy conditions) and begins the sentence with 'then,' but omits the conclusion (e.g., an inequality or explicit claim about the OOD loss ratio). The subsequent sentence begins with 'This holds...' without a clear referent, suggesting a missing line or equation was lost during OCR or formatting. A reader attempting to understand the theorem statement from this text would find it incomplete.

comments.title: OCR Artifacts in Proposition 3.6 Math Block
comments.quote: _∀t,L_ood(_B_ft(_t_)_⊤v_ft(_t_))_>_0_, if v_0|_∼N_(0_,σ_2_I_)_is randomly initialized (FT),_|(3.10)|
comments.explanation: The mathematical notation in the table snippet for Proposition 3.6 is heavily corrupted by OCR (e.g., `v_0|_∼N_(0_,σ_2_I_)_is`, `B_ft(t)_⊤v_ft(t)`). While the logical content (random initialization leads to positive OOD error) is clear from context, the notation is inconsistent and illegible, which frustrates the precise reading of the statement.

comments.title: Ambiguous Usage of 'Local Minimum'
comments.quote: and that fine-tuning converges to a local minimum of its loss, then fine-tuning does better ID almost surely
comments.explanation: In the overparameterized linear setting analyzed in this section, the training loss is convex in the parameters (v, B). Gradient descent converges to a global minimizer (or minimizers, which are technically local minima but connected by rotations). The phrasing 'converges to a local minimum' is technically true but potentially misleading, as it obscures the fact that the algorithm is finding the *optimal* solution for the trained data distribution rather than just 'stopping' at a local optimum.

comments.title: Incorrect Average OOD Scores in 4.3
comments.quote: As expected, fine-tuning does improve a little, but linear probing (average accuracy: 67.1%) is still better than fine-tuning (average accuracy: 61.3%).
comments.explanation: The paragraph immediately preceding this statement presents the summary averages from Table 2 as 66.2% for linear probing (LP) and 59.3% for fine-tuning (FT). The values 67.1% and 61.3% appear to be typos or hallucinations of the correct figures. Presenting average scores that do not match the table and the summary provided right before them misleads the reader about the experimental outcome of the early stopping experiment. The correct numbers (approx. 66.2 vs 59.3) support the claim that LP outperforms FT on OOD accuracy.

comments.title: Incorrect Numerical Factor Claim
comments.quote: ...average distance changed by LP-FT both ID (0.0011 ± 0.0001) and OOD (0.0009 ± 0.0001) is 20 × smaller than for fine-tuning.
comments.explanation: Based on the provided standard deviations, the ratio of distances is approximately 17.1 (0.0188 / 0.0011) for ID data and 18.6 (0.0167 / 0.0009) for OOD data. Neither of these ratios equals 20. While the text implies the LP-FT change is 'roughly' or 'significantly' smaller, asserting a precise factor of 20 is technically incorrect based on the stated data points.

comments.title: Discrepancy in Count of 'Best' ID Results
comments.quote: This is true for 5/6 ID and 10/10 OOD datasets—every dataset except FMoW ID, where it is in between linear probing and fine-tuning.
comments.explanation: Table 1 shows that LP-FT achieves 51.8% ID accuracy on FMoW, which is strictly lower than the fine-tuning baseline of 56.5%. Therefore, LP-FT is not the best performing method on FMoW ID. While LP-FT is tied with FT on ImageNet ID, it is not strictly better there either. Counting this as 'best' implies LP-FT performed best on at least 5 of the 6 datasets. Given the tie and the loss, the count of 5/6 is ambiguous and potentially incorrect depending on whether 'best' includes ties or strict suprema.

comments.title: Citation Label Typo (Section C)
comments.quote: See Section C for additional related work on theory of overparameterized models.
comments.explanation: The passage is located at the end of the paper (concluding remarks/citations section). The paper structure revealed in the context is: Introduction (1), Setup (2), Theory (3), Conclusion. Section numbers 1, 2, and 3 are present. 'Section C' is not a section in the main body of this paper. Given the context mentions 'Appendix A' for proofs, it is highly likely that 'Section C' is a typo for 'Appendix C' (the standard location for related work appendices). While this does not affect the mathematical validity or the core argument, it confuses the reader about where to look for this information.

comments.title: Notational Inconsistency (LPFT)
comments.quote: LPFT could be useful in other situations, for example in CLIP we could initialize the final layer with the zero-shot classifier and then fine-tune the entire model, as done in concurrent work (Wortsman et al., 2021). In NLP, linear probing is not as good—here we could first prompt-tune (Lester et al., 2021) and then fine-tune the entire model.
comments.explanation: Throughout the provided context (e.g., Abstract, Theory, Propositions), the authors consistently use the hyphenated notation 'LP-FT' (Linear Probing then Fine-Tuning). In this specific sentence within the Conclusion, the acronym appears without hyphens: 'LPFT'. This appears to be a typo or OCR artifact. Standard practice in academic writing is to maintain notation consistency. The meaning is clear ('Linear Probing then Fine-Tuning'), but the inconsistency makes the text slightly less polished.
